references.name: Marimekko
references.takeaway: Use bold repeating geometric patterns (circles, stripes, chevrons) as background fills for alternating content sections, creating a textile-patchwork rhythm down the page.

howApplied.element: Geometric SVG pattern backgrounds (6 patterns: triangles, circles, diamonds, zigzag, stripes, dots)
howApplied.description: Every card, column header, and content section uses one of six repeating geometric SVG patterns as a background fill via data-pattern attributes. These patterns tile like Marimekko fabric prints, creating the patchwork effect across the entire interface. Each pattern is encoded as inline SVG in CSS for zero HTTP requests.

Six stat cards are arranged in a 3-column grid with intentional rotation angles and offsets, each with a different pattern background and accent color border. The overlapping, slightly-rotated arrangement creates the patchwork textile rhythm Marimekko achieves in their product grid layouts.

Each idea card has a unique geometric pattern strip at the top, creating a grid where every card has distinct visual identity through its pattern — directly inspired by Marimekko's product thumbnails inheriting fabric patterns as backgrounds.

howApplied.element: Strong primary-color palette with zero pastels or gradients
howApplied.description: The palette uses fully saturated coral (#e05a4f), olive (#6b8e23), gold (#d4a843), and teal (#2a9d8f) with no pastel variants, softened tints, or gradients. This direct, bold color usage mirrors Marimekko's commitment to strong primary colors.

references.name: Hermes
references.takeaway: Introduce hand-illustrated decorative borders and divider elements between sections — line-art motifs that feel drawn by hand rather than generated, echoing textile print registration marks.

howApplied.description: Every quilt patch section has a decorative seam border at its top edge — a repeating linear gradient alternating between coral, olive, gold, and teal segments. These seams function like the hand-crafted registration marks between textile print runs, separating content sections with artisanal precision.

howApplied.element: Ornamental SVG dividers in AI chat
howApplied.description: Each chat message is separated by an inline SVG ornamental divider featuring circles, diamonds, rectangles, and connecting lines in accent colors. These feel hand-illustrated, like the decorative borders in Hermes editorial storytelling layouts. Each divider uses a different sequence of geometric shapes.

The active navigation item has a decorative geometric border at its bottom — a repeating pattern of coral, olive, and gold segments that echoes textile print registration marks rather than a simple solid underline.

The mood board features hand-drawn-style SVG connector elements with dashed curves, geometric shapes at endpoints (circles, triangles, diamonds), and folk-art motifs that feel sketched rather than programmatically generated.

howApplied.element: Cream and ivory canvas with signature accent
howApplied.description: Following Hermes' approach of using cream/white as the canvas with a single strong brand accent, this design uses ivory #faf5eb as the base with coral as the primary signature accent, while olive, gold, and teal provide supporting roles.

references.name: Cooper Hewitt Smithsonian Design Museum
references.takeaway: Use a variable-size mosaic grid where featured items span 2 or 3 columns and secondary items fill 1 column, creating the quilt-like compositional rhythm of a textile sample book.

howApplied.description: The exhibition collection grid uses three card sizes — large (2-column span), medium (1-column), and small (1-column) — arranged in a 3-column grid. Featured exhibitions get the large treatment while secondary shows fill smaller spaces, creating the mosaic rhythm of Cooper Hewitt's collection grid with its hero 2x-width items.

Project cards display multiple metadata dimensions simultaneously — medium type, work count, gallery location, and status — echoing Cooper Hewitt's collection grid that shows era, material, origin, and designer on each item card. Kanban cards show geometric status markers, assignees, and category tags.

howApplied.element: Dashboard stat cards as variable-sized collage
howApplied.description: The dashboard's overlapping stat cards at different angles and sizes create a variable-density composition similar to Cooper Hewitt's mixed-size collection grid, where the visual hierarchy is established through size and position rather than strict grid alignment.

Each entity card in the schema planner has a geometric type badge (circle for Collection, triangle for Asset, square for Person, pentagon for Document) with associated accent colors — a taxonomy visualization approach inspired by Cooper Hewitt's rich metadata-driven collection browsing.
